title: Git Basics: Version Control from Scratch
description: Learn Git the way professional developers use it — tracking changes, collaborating safely, and never losing work again. Every lesson uses real scenarios because version control matters most when the stakes are real.

lessons.title: Initialize a Repository & Make Your First Commit
objectives: Understand what version control is and why it matters
Initialize a new Git repository with git init
Create files, stage them with git add, and commit with git commit
hints: git init creates a hidden .git folder — that's where Git stores the entire history of your project
git add <file> stages a file (marks it for the next commit). git add . stages everything
git commit -m 'Your message' saves a snapshot. Write messages that explain WHY, not just what
assessmentCriteria: Student can explain the difference between a working directory and a repository
A new Git repository is initialized successfully
At least one file is created, staged, and committed
Commit message is descriptive and meaningful

lessons.title: The Staging Area & Reading Status
objectives: Understand the three states of a file: untracked, staged, and committed
Use git status and git diff to inspect changes before committing
Selectively stage files to create focused, meaningful commits
hints: git diff shows unstaged changes. git diff --staged shows what's about to be committed
You don't have to stage everything at once — stage related changes together for cleaner history
git restore --staged <file> removes a file from staging without losing your changes
assessmentCriteria: Student can read git status output and explain each section
Student uses git diff to review changes before staging
Multiple files are committed in separate, logical commits
Student demonstrates unstaging a file with git restore

lessons.title: Creating & Switching Branches
objectives: Understand branches as parallel timelines of development
Create new branches with git branch and switch with git checkout or git switch
Work on a feature branch without affecting the main branch
hints: git switch -c feature-name creates a new branch AND switches to it in one command
Branches are cheap in Git — create one for every feature, bug fix, or experiment
git log --oneline --graph --all shows all branches visually in the terminal
assessmentCriteria: Student creates at least two branches besides main
Student makes commits on a feature branch without affecting main
Student can switch between branches and observe different file states
Student uses git log --graph to visualize branch history

lessons.title: Merging Branches & Resolving Conflicts
objectives: Merge feature branches back into main with git merge
Resolve merge conflicts when two branches edit the same file
hints: Always switch to the branch you want to merge INTO first (usually main), then run git merge <branch>
Conflict markers look like <<<<<<< HEAD ... ======= ... >>>>>>> branch-name — keep the code you want and delete the markers
After resolving conflicts, stage the fixed files and commit to complete the merge
assessmentCriteria: Student successfully merges at least one feature branch into main
Student creates and resolves at least one merge conflict
Student can explain the difference between fast-forward and three-way merges
Final git log shows a clean merge history
